item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The third collapsible being outside of a quote block was slightly off to me; that can be placed in a footnote while keeping the image fine by changing the following:

 [[include component:image-block name=...

to

 [[include component:image-block-quoted name=...

And then putting the other lines into quote blocks.

item.title: Incident Report 102 and SCP-682
item.content:encoded:
I might be missing something about SCP-682, but isn't it extraordinarily noteworthy that 2 of them were able to be terminated by MTF Nu-7 (even though they used Mark 77 incendiary bombs)?
As far as I can tell otherwise, SCP-682 is meant to be unkillable.
They then mention they neutralized the third instance, but there is no elaboration. The way the incident report is written, it isn't reasonable to infer that SCP-682 is this third instance, so then what happened to the third instance?

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

This article already links to SCP-682 and SCP-2082 which are fantastic as far as wikiwalk is concerned, and I'd normally be fine leaving that alone. However, the mention of MTF Nu-7 can be turned into an inline link to that groups page on the MTF list, and such a change is mainly non-invasive, so I'm going to propose it. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

Members of MTF λ-6 utilized vehicles to lure 2 of the 3 SCP-2706-2 instances into a designated kill zone manned by MTF Nu-7 (Hammer Down). MTF Nu-7 was able to terminate both instances by utilizing Mark 77 incendiary bombs.

To this:

Members of MTF λ-6 utilized vehicles to lure 2 of the 3 SCP-2706-2 instances into a designated kill zone manned by MTF Nu-7 (Hammer Down). MTF Nu-7 was able to terminate both instances by utilizing Mark 77 incendiary bombs.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: Hmmm...
item.content:encoded: Why did my mind immediately jump to Godzilla with the last inactive growth?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Much better. Good job. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I will agree that any mammoth found in the modern day will be studied by the Foundation regardless of circumstances, since mammoths aren't supposed to be around nowadays (and it is a "product" of an SCP in this case, so it's worthy studying).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Forget then, the chances of them being related. Wouldn't the foundation like to compare their differences if they aren't related?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Since 2082 does have anomalous properties, it stands to assume that they would occur if revived. Perhaps they will take the mammoth for research purposes, but saying that it may have a connection to 2082 is slightly "jumping to the conclusion" for me.

Overall, my issue would be a wonky cross-reference that may be valid, but is hard to swallow.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the way this escalates, but I feel like we've already got one or more things that produce normal animals except, oh, now it's making humans and/or weird stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Standardized it. There is no good reason for diversion from the format.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd honestly like the radiation more if it wasn't associated with "more danger," and was just "strange species that's radioactive."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Any reason the image formatting isn't the standard format used by other articles?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Piggybacking on this to note that 99% of all species that have ever lived are extinct, so the breakdown of what it's spat out is doubly strange. I agree about the anomalocaris thing, but the majority of what this spits out is probably going to be unknown to science, if it can draw from anything that's lived on earth.

I also agree with Scantron and Mann, that the danger feels tacked on. The last one being radioactive displeases me, as does the stated link between gestation time and danger posed. The former strikes me as uninspired: you wanted some way to show, "This is totally whack!" but went with a completely mundane physical process, which, granted, is definitely unexpected in a living animal, but overused on the site, I feel. There's so many other things you could go with that would serve the same purpose. You could have watches run slower in proximity. You could have the air thicken into a supercritical fluid around it, in defiance of the physical properties which govern such. But a trunk full of Marie Curie is boring. Or maybe not, if the implication is that man with a knowledge of nuclear forces is the most dangerous thing known, but then that's "the monster is man" trope, which kind of is.

As to the latter, of gestation periods apparently corresponding to danger, this bothers me because of how utterly arbitrary it is. You can't really look at what's presented here and ask, "Why do dangerous things take longer?" and come up with any other answer than we're supposed to be afraid of the last radioactive thing, which hearkens back to the earlier bits about the predictable and uninteresting escalation of danger.

I'd also like to see some of the why of this thing hinted at, if there is any, so it's not just a tree that spits out fauna which spawn slower if dangerous because reasons.

So, in short, this could be good. Presently, I wouldn't say it's bad; not executed as well as it could be, and as Scan says, not particularly distinct from other monster spawning things. Would gladly reread if critiques raised here are given consideration.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Finally, I liked that the human they found was made a researcher. That was a nice touch, I thought.

Huh. Didn't even notice that. Good eye, Mann.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the basic idea here. However, I feel you could do a lot more fun stuff with the -02 instances. For one thing, look at what the actual distribution of species for different phyla breaks out to. Did you know that one in four species is a beetle (and one in eleven is a weevil)? Also think into more interesting extinct species. There's a mammal. Why not something from the Burgess Shale? An anomalocaris would raise some eyebrows. What if it summoned species that don't exist yet?

I felt the "summons black-box SCP that's horridangerous) felt a bit tacked-on and predictable. I would look for an actual SCP that's an anomalous species and use that.

Finally, I liked that the human they found was made a researcher. That was a nice touch, I thought.

No vote, since I like the idea, but if you can use the animal-generation in a more interesting, creative manner, this will become an easy upvote for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A lot of people whose opinions I set my clocks by really like this SCP. But I don't. :[

I found all of it predictable except the use of Jeremy Monroe, which is essentially a footnote to the story. (Not a bad thing, by the way.) I was especially hoping for more stuff, and more interesting stuff, due to the use of collapsibles (though I know that's a meta concern).

This is very well-written and competently executed, but it's generic.

-1, but I'd be happy to see whatever the author writes next.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"This implies that not all mammoths are SCP-2082."

So finding out if a mammoth we have on hand is related in some way doesn't make sense to you?

To me it's perfectly reasonable to mention that the foundation is comparing the two. Why wouldn't they?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I tend to agree that it was somewhat generic and similar to things already on the site.

I figured the cross-link was appropriate because if I was a Foundation biologist with appropriate clearance and a woolly mammoth popped out of a tree, I'd have a heart-attack.

Ultimately, I upvoted when you made the plant guy a researcher. That was just neat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Are you sure about the 44 cm/g^3?

Obsolete … Scantron saved the day :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Like Decibelles, I am not so sure about the reference to 2082. From 2082's page,

SCP-2082 is an extinct subspecies of the animal known as the woolly mammoth

This implies that not all mammoths are SCP-2082. For all we know, it could be a normal mammoth. Unless there was anomalous phenomena occurring at that time that is uncannily similar to 2082's behaviour…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Frankly, I'm inclined to agree that this is very predictable — that is, all of the twists here feel like stock footage, and the escalation is essentially by-the-numbers. The anomaly itself is another "random X generator" (hell, for animals we have SCP-646 already), which isn't bad on its own, but does not help the originality problem at all. It comes from nowhere and escalates predictably, having no sort of deeper story to it that I can discern.

I'm not seeing anything of value here. -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The article was well written, and I found the crosslink to be very appropriate, but I could guess exactly what would happen once I read non-collapsible part. I knew that there was gonna be at least 2 of the following:
-Human
—Possibly a foundation member
-Dinosaur or old creature
-SCP (the fact that it was hidden was kinda boring in my opinion)

And I was correct, although I didn't guess the very last addendum. The fact that I was checking a mental list of rather common tropes didn't leave me with a good impression of the article. No-vote from me, because while I didn't enjoy the article, it isn't bad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the piece but I'm holding my vote because there are some minor suggestions that I believe would improve the quality of the SCP.

Addendum 1 gives, in my opinion, too much information about the particular instance of SCP-2706-2. Disclousing less information could make the Addendum even more interesting. On the same vein, usually locations of the SCP are ██ so making the location so visible makes me wonder, do we need to know where it is? Couldn’t that be considered a security breach? Especially considering it is classified as a Keter object.

Which brings us to, should it be consider a Keter object over an Euclid one? I mean, it doesn’t seem to me as “an inimical threat to the safety of Foundation personnel and the rest of mankind and either require extensive and complex procedures to contain or cannot be fully contained by the Foundation's current technology and knowledge.”

Now, instead of giving a list of animals that have been produced by the SCP, why not put them in a log? It would give it a closer approach to the usual SCP format. Also, was this SCP “recovered”? Wouldn´t something like “fisrt came to The Foundation attention when …” fit better to the nature of the SCP?

Finally, no amnesics to the authorities that found two cheetahs and a dolphin in a US forest?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My first thought when I started reading it was "why Keter?" but I was sold by the second half. I feel like this has great potential to turn into something more. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I read an earlier version - didn't like it, but couldn't work out why. Turns out replacing the former O5 discussion with the new final addendum = upvote. Nice work - a great example of a small change bringing everything together.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is actually really fun and creative. Nice work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Heyo. I fixed up your image locations. You uploaded them to the SCP Series 3 page and not to this page. I can't delete the old ones but right now all the images are localized properly. As for the image, I don't know of a good way to fix the image clipping problem, so I've removed the box around the 3rd collapsible's material.

I was glad to lend my co-author to this. It seemed like a solid idea and I'm glad it's finally up. ;)

Edit: And congratulations on your first SCP. Great work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I still think it's missing something, though I can't explain what that something is. But it is well written and I can't find anything wrong with it, so no vote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One of the images was broken. I localized it.

As for the piece itself, I like it. It's short and simple, but in a good way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The story. The way you start with a single conceit and keep growing it larger and larger—that's perfect.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Terrible at coding using a phone. Fixing the photo ASAP
EDIT: Picture is up but I think it's clipping, anyone know how to fix?
EDIT EDIT: Pic fixed

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It does make sense, though I wasn't quite able to pick up on the connection at first.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Foundation had a mammoth with anomalous abilities that remained unexplained. This is a mammoth that appears normal but has an anomalous origin. It does make sense for them to try to compare them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Liked it on the draft forum, still like it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting idea, good example of crosslinking, and your last image doesn't work. 2/3 = +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is really interesting (and I'll read it through again later), but I'm not quite clear on the 2082 connection.

Your image in the third collapsible doesn't work, by the way.

EDIT: I'm not a fan of how the addena are formed and structured though. Really, only the last addendum seems like it needs to be hidden by security credentials.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Huge thanks to Dr. Cimmerian for the writing assistance!
